title: Entity Selection: Advising the Client
topic: Choosing among C corp, S corp, partnership, and sole proprietorship based on client facts

scenarios.title: The reinvesting founder
scenarios.client_brief: Maya is launching a SaaS company. She plans to raise venture capital within 18 months, reinvest all profits for the foreseeable future, and eventually sell. She has two co-founders and wants to grant equity to early employees.

client.name: Maya Chen
client.quote: I'm starting a SaaS company with two co-founders. We expect to raise venture capital within about 18 months, and we want to reinvest all of our profits back into the business for the foreseeable future, then eventually sell. I'd also love to grant equity to our earliest employees. How should we set up the entity?

factors.label: VC / entity investors
factors.note: S corps can't have entity or nonresident owners — a VC fund voids the election the day it invests.

factors.label: QSBS exit potential
factors.note: Reinvest-then-sell is the textbook §1202 setup; a C corp can exclude a large share of gain on a qualifying sale.

factors.label: Reinvesting all profits
factors.note: Double taxation only bites on distributions — defer those and the second layer largely sleeps.

factors.label: Employee equity
factors.note: Profits interests are flexible in a partnership; the single-class-of-stock rule constrains S corps.

feedback.ccorp: Right call. The VC raise alone rules out an S corp, and reinvest-then-sell is the §1202 pattern.
feedback.scorp: The classic trap — the moment a VC fund invests, the election is void, and you've forfeited the QSBS exit.
feedback.partnership: Not wrong, but you leave §1202 on the table and VCs strongly prefer to fund C corps.

scenarios.title: Debt-financed losses
scenarios.client_brief: Three investors are buying and renovating an apartment building, financed mostly with a bank mortgage. They expect large depreciation and operating losses in the early years and want to use those losses on their own returns. All three materially participate in real estate.

client.name: Daniel Reyes
client.quote: Two partners and I are buying an apartment building and renovating it, financed mostly with a bank mortgage. We're expecting large depreciation and operating losses in the early years, and we'd really like to use those losses on our own personal returns. All three of us actively work in real estate. What's the right entity for us?

factors.label: Entity debt creates basis
factors.note: Partners add their share of qualified nonrecourse debt to outside basis — S corp shareholders only get basis for loans they personally make.

factors.label: Special allocations
factors.note: §704(b) lets the partnership steer losses to the partners who can use them; S corps must allocate strictly by ownership.

factors.label: Losses usable by owners
factors.note: Passthrough losses flow to the owners; a C corp would trap them at the entity.

factors.label: Passive loss / at-risk limits
factors.note: §469 and §465 can still cap what owners deduct regardless of entity — a real caveat, not a tiebreaker.

feedback.partnership: Yes — debt basis plus §704(b) allocations is exactly why losses-with-leverage favors a partnership.
feedback.scorp: An S corp passes losses through, but shareholders can't count the mortgage in basis, so the deductions stall.
feedback.ccorp: A C corp traps the losses at the entity level — the owners get nothing to use on their own returns.

scenarios.title: Trimming self-employment tax
scenarios.client_brief: Devon runs a profitable marketing agency netting about $200K a year as a sole proprietor. The business isn't a specified service trade for QBI purposes and he has no plans to sell. He's tired of paying self-employment tax on every dollar.

client.name: Devon Park
client.quote: My marketing agency nets around two hundred thousand dollars a year, and right now I'm just a sole proprietor. We're not a specified service business for the QBI rules, and I have no plans to sell. Honestly, I'm just tired of paying self-employment tax on every single dollar. Is there a better structure for me?

factors.label: Wage vs. distribution split
factors.note: An S corp pays reasonable W-2 wages (payroll tax) and takes the rest as distributions (no SE/payroll tax).

factors.label: Profit high enough to matter
factors.note: At ~$200K the SE-tax savings comfortably clear the cost of payroll and a separate return.

factors.label: Reasonable-comp exposure
factors.note: Set wages too low and the IRS recharacterizes distributions as wages — the savings have a ceiling.

factors.label: QBI wage interaction
factors.note: S corp wages reduce the QBI base; for a non-SSTB this is usually a minor offset to the SE-tax win.

feedback.scorp: Right — at this profit level the reasonable-comp split is the standard SE-tax play, watch the comp.
feedback.soleprop: Simplest, but at $200K you're leaving real SE-tax savings on the table for no offsetting benefit.
feedback.ccorp: A C corp adds double taxation and kills QBI to solve a problem the S corp solves more cleanly.

scenarios.title: Don't over-engineer it
scenarios.client_brief: Priya just left her job to consult on the side. She expects to net around $35K this first year and isn't sure it'll grow. A colleague told her to 'set up an S corp to save on taxes.' She wants the simplest setup that doesn't waste money.

client.name: Priya Anand
client.quote: I just left my job to do some consulting on the side. I'll probably net around thirty-five thousand dollars this first year, but I honestly don't know if it'll grow. A friend told me I should set up an S corp to save on taxes. I really just want the simplest setup that doesn't waste money. What do you recommend?

factors.label: Profit too low to justify cost
factors.note: At ~$35K, SE-tax savings from an S corp are small and easily swallowed by payroll and 1120-S prep costs.

factors.label: Administrative overhead
factors.note: An S corp means payroll filings, reasonable comp, and a separate return — real cost and complexity.

factors.label: Unproven, may not grow
factors.note: Electing too early commits her to overhead before the income justifies it; she can elect later.

feedback.soleprop: Right — the fancy answer isn't always the correct one. At $35K, simple wins; she can revisit as profit grows.
feedback.scorp: This is the 'always be an S corp' myth. The overhead outweighs the savings at this profit level.
feedback.ccorp: Far too much structure for a small, unproven consulting business — double tax and complexity for no gain.
